key_points: The essay is written by Helen Keller, who was both blind and deaf.
She asks the readers to imagine what they would do if they were struck blind for only three days.
Her main message is that people with sight often 'see' very little and take their senses for granted.
She encourages everyone to live each day as if they might lose their sight or hearing tomorrow.
The text is an appeal to appreciate the beauty of the world around us through all our senses.
examples: Day 1: She would want to see the faces of her friends, her teacher (Anne Sullivan), and simple things like a warm rug and books.
Day 2: She would visit museums to see the history of the world and art, and watch a sunrise.
Day 3: She would spend time in the busy city, watching people in their daily lives and seeing a play or movie.
She mentions how she 'sees' a flower by touching its petals or feels the vibration of a bird singing.
She describes the 'miracle' of the woods that people with sight often walk through without noticing anything.
formulas_or_processes: The 'Use Your Senses' Rule → Smell the perfume of flowers, taste every morsel, touch with joy, and hear the music of the world.
Perspective Shift → Instead of focusing on what we don't have, notice the 'glories' of what we do have.
The 3-Day Plan → 1. People/Home, 2. History/Nature, 3. The Present/Daily Life.
exam_tips: Notice the irony: A blind person is teaching sighted people how to truly 'see'.
Be ready to list what she would do on each of the three days.
